elements.sent-2411-001: Latent space optimization (LSO) is a powerful method for designing discrete, high-dimensional biological sequences that maximize expensive black-box functions, such as wet lab experiments.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is accomplished by learning a latent space from available data and using a surrogate model $\surrogate$ to guide optimization algorithms toward optimal outputs.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing methods struggle when labeled data is limited, as training $\surrogate$ with few labeled data points can lead to subpar outputs, offering no advantage over the training data itself.
elements.sent-2411-004: We address this challenge by introducing \method, a GRaph-based Latent SmOOThing for Biological Sequence Optimization.
elements.sent-2411-005: In particular, \method generates pseudo-labels for neighbors sampled around the training latent embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-006: These pseudo-labels are then refined and smoothed by Label Propagation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we theoretically and empirically justify our approach, demonstrate \methodname's ability to extrapolate to regions beyond the training set while maintaining reliability within an upper bound of their expected distances from the training regions.
elements.sent-2411-008: We evaluate \method on various biological sequence design tasks, including protein optimization (GFP and AAV) and three tasks with exact oracles from Design-Bench.
elements.sent-2411-009: The results demonstrate that \method equalizes and surpasses existing methods without requiring access to black-box oracles or vast amounts of labeled data, highlighting its practicality and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-011: Proteins are crucial biomolecules that play diverse and essential roles in every living organism.
elements.sent-2411-012: Enhancing protein functions or cellular fitness is vital for industrial, research, and therapeutic applications \cite{Huang2016, doi:10.1021/acs.chemrev.1c00260 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: One powerful approach to achieve this is directed evolution, which involves iteratively performing random mutations and screening for proteins with desired phenotypes \cite{ARNOLD19965091 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, the vast space of possible proteins makes exhaustive searches impractical in nature, the laboratory, or computationally.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, recent machine learning (ML) methods have been developed to improve the sample efficiency of this evolutionary search \cite{pmlr-v162-jain22a, pmlr-v162-ren22a, lee2024robust .}
elements.sent-2411-016: When experimental data is available, these approaches use a surrogate model $\surrogate$, trained to guide optimization algorithms toward optimal outputs.
elements.sent-2411-017: While these methods have achieved state-of-the-art results on various benchmarks, most neglect the scenario of extremely limited labeled data and fail to utilize the abundant unlabeled data.
elements.sent-2411-018: This is a significant issue because exploring protein function and characteristics through iterative mutation in wet-lab is costly \cite{Fowler2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: As later shown in \Cref{sec:numerical-results , previous works perform poorly in this context, proving that training $\surrogate$ with few labeled data points can lead to subpar designs, offering no advantage over the training data itself.}
elements.sent-2411-020: One potential solution for dealing with noisy and limited data is to regularize the fitness landscape model, smoothing the sequence representation and facilitating the use of gradient-based optimization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-021: Although some works have addressed this problem (Section \ref{sec:related ), none can optimize effectively in the case of labeled scarcity.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our work builds on the observation by \citet{pmlr-v162-trabucco22a that surrogate models $\surrogate$ trained on limited labeled data are vulnerable to noisy labels.}
elements.sent-2411-023: This sensitivity can lead the model to sample false negatives or become trapped in suboptimal solutions (local minima) when used for guiding offline optimization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-024: To address this issue, the ultimate objective is to enhance the predictive ability of $\surrogate$, reducing the risk of finding suboptimal solutions.
elements.sent-2411-025: To fulfill this, we propose \method, a novel framework designed to tackle the problem of labeled scarcity by generating synthetic sample representations from existing data.
elements.sent-2411-026: From the features encoded by the encoder, we formulate sequences as a graph with fitness values as node attributes and apply Label Propagation \cite{NIPS2003_87682805 to generate labels for newly created nodes synthesized through interpolation within existing nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The smoothed data is then fitted with a neural network for optimization.
elements.sent-2411-028: Figure \ref{fig:method_overview provides an overview of our framework.}
elements.sent-2411-029: We evaluate our method on two fitness optimization tasks: Green Fluorescent Proteins (GFP) \cite{sarkisyan2016local and Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV) \cite{bryant2021deep .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Our results show that \method outperforms previous state-of-the-art baselines across all difficulties of both tasks.
elements.sent-2411-031: We also demonstrate that our method performs stably in extreme cases where there are fewer than 100 labeled training sequences, unlike other approaches.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We introduce \method, a novel framework that uses graph-based smoothing to train a smoothed fitness model, which is then used in the optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-034: We theoretically show that our smoothing technique can expand into extrapolation regions while keeping a reasonable distance from the training data.
elements.sent-2411-035: This helps to reduce errors occurring when the surrogate model makes predictions for unseen points too far from the training set.
elements.sent-2411-036: We empirically show that \method achieves state-of-the-art results across all difficulties in two protein design benchmarks, AAV and GFP.
elements.sent-2411-037: Notably, in extreme cases with very limited labeled data, our approach performs stably, achieving $6$ fold fitness improvement in GFP and $1.3$ times higher in AAV compared to the training set.
elements.sent-2411-038: We further evaluate \method on diverse tasks of different domains (e.g., robotics, DNA) within the Design-Bench.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our experimental results are competitive with state-of-the-art approaches, highlighting the method's domain-agnostic capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-040: Directed evolution is a traditional paradigm in protein sequence design that has achieved notable successes \cite{https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201708408 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Within this framework, various machine learning algorithms have been proposed to improve the sample efficiency of evolutionary searches \cite{pmlr-v162-ren22a, Qiu_2022, Emami2023, Tran2023.11.28.568945 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, most of these methods optimize protein sequences directly in the sequence space, dealing with discrete, high-dimensional decision variables.
elements.sent-2411-043: Alternatively, \citet{doi:10.1021/acscentsci.7b00572 and \citet{Castro2022 employed a VAE model and applied gradient ascent to optimize the latent representation, which is then decoded into biological sequences.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Similarly, \citet{lee2023protein used an off-policy reinforcement learning method to facilitate updates in the representation space.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Another notable approach \cite{pmlr-v162-stanton22a involves training a denoising autoencoder with a discriminative multi-task Gaussian process head, enabling gradient-based optimization of multi-objective acquisition functions in the latent space of the autoencoder.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Recently, latent diffusion has been introduced for designing novel proteins, leveraging the capabilities of a protein language model \cite{chen2023ampdiffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The NK model was an early effort to represent protein fitness smoothness using a statistical model of epistasis \cite{Kauffman1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Additionally, \citet{frey2024protein proposed to learn a smoothed energy function, allowing sequences to be sampled from the smoothed data manifold using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC).}
elements.sent-2411-049: Currently, GGS \cite{kirjner2024improving is most related to our work by utilizing discrete regularization using graph-based smoothing techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, GGS enforces similar sequences to have similar fitness, potentially leading to suboptimal performance since fitness can change significantly with a single mutation \cite{doi:10.1073/pnas.2109649118 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Our work addresses this by encoding sequences into the latent space and constructing a graph based on the similarity of latent vectors.
elements.sent-2411-052: Furthermore, since our framework operates in the latent space, it is not constrained by biological domains and can be applied to other fields like robotics, as demonstrated in \Cref{app:design-bench .}
elements.sent-2411-053: We begin this section by formally defining the problem.
elements.sent-2411-054: Next, we detail the construction of the protein latent space and present our proposed latent graph-based smoothing framework, \methodname.
elements.sent-2411-055: We address the challenge of designing proteins to find high-fitness sequences $s$ within the sequence space $\mathcal{A ^L$, where $\mathcal{A}$ represents the amino acid vocabulary (i.e. $|\mathcal{A | = 20$ since both animal and plant proteins are made up of about 20 common amino acids) and $L$ is the desired sequence length.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Our goal is to design sequences that maximize a black-box protein fitness function $\mathcal{O : \mathcal{A ^L \mapsto \mathbb{R}$, which can only be evaluated through wet-lab experiments: \begin{equation}s^{*} = \underset{s \in \mathcal{A}^L}{\text{argmax}} ~\mathcal{O}(s).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: For in-silico evaluation, given a static dataset of all known sequences and fitness measurements $\mathcal{D ^{* = \{(s, y) | s \in \mathcal{A ^L, y \in \mathbb{R}\}$, an oracle $\mathcal O_\psi$ parameterized by $\psi$ is trained to minimize the prediction error on $\mathcal{D ^*$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Afterward, $\mathcal{O _\psi$ is used as an approximator for the black-box function $\mathcal{O}$ to evaluate computational methods that are developed on a training subset $\mathcal{D}$ of $\mathcal{D ^{*}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In other words, given $\mathcal{D}$, our task is to generate sequences $\hat{s}$ that optimize the fitness approximated by $\mathcal{O _\psi$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: To do this, we train a surrogate model $f$ on $\mathcal{D}$ and use it to guide the search for optimal candidates, which are later evaluated by $\mathcal{O _\psi$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: This setup is similar to work done by \citet{kirjner2024improving, pmlr-v162-jain22a .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure/framework.pdf} \caption{Overall framework of \methodname. After encoding sequences into the latent space, we generate new samples by adding Gaussian noise to existing vectors. These synthetic data lie outside the training set's convex hull but within a reliable zone, as their distances from the hull are below a certain upper bound. We construct a kNN graph and run label propagation to smooth and refine node labels. These nodes and their fitness values are then used to train the surrogate model, which is subsequently employed for optimization.} \label{fig:method_overview} \Description{Overall framework of \methodname.}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Constructing Latent Space of Protein Sequences
elements.sent-2411-062: Unsupervised learning has achieved remarkable success in domains like natural language processing \cite{gpt and computer vision \cite{sam .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This method efficiently learns data representations by identifying underlying patterns without requiring labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-064: The label-free nature of unsupervised learning aligns well with protein design challenges, where experimental fitness evaluations are costly, while unlabeled sequences are abundant.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this work, considering a dataset of unlabeled sequences of a protein family $s \in \mathcal{A ^ L$ denoted as $\mathcal{D _u = \{s_i\}_{i=1 ^{N_u}$, we train a VAE comprising an encoder $\phi: {\mathcal{A ^ L \mapsto \mathbb{R ^ {d_h}$ and a decoder $\theta: \mathbb{R ^d \mapsto \mathcal{A ^ L$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Each sequence $s_i$ is encoded into a low-dimensional vector $h_i \in \mathbb{R ^ {d_h}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Subsequently, the mean $\mu_i \in \mathbb{R ^ d$ and log-variance $\log \sigma_i \in \mathbb{R ^ d$ of the variational posterior approximation are computed from $h_i$ using two feed-forward networks, $\mu_\phi: \mathbb{R ^ {d_h \mapsto \mathbb{R ^ d$ and $\sigma_\phi: \mathbb{R ^ {d_h \mapsto \mathbb{R ^ d$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: A latent vector $x_i \in \mathbb{R ^ d$ is then sampled from the Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N (\mu_i, \sigma_i ^ 2)$, and the decoder $\theta$ maps $x_i$ back to the reconstructed protein sequence $\hat{s _i \in \mathcal{A ^ L$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The training objective involves the cross-entropy loss $\mathcal{C (\hat{s _i, s_i)$ between the ground truth sequence $s_i$ and the generated $\hat s_i$, as well as the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between $\mathcal{N (\mu_i, \sigma_i ^ 2)$ and $\mathcal{N (0, I_d)$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{vae} = \frac{1}{N_u}\sum_{i=1} ^ {N_u} \mathcal{C}(\hat{s}_i, s_i) + \frac{\eta}{N_u} \sum_{i=1} ^ {N_u} D_{\text{KL}} (\mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma_i ^ 2) \| \mathcal{N}(0, I_d)). \label{eq:vae_loss}\end{equation} Here, $\eta$ is the hyperparameter to control the disentanglement property of the VAE's latent space.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: A critical distinction must be made between the latent space of protein sequences and the protein fitness landscape.
elements.sent-2411-071: While training a VAE-based model, we do not access protein fitness scores, but we learn their continuous representations.
elements.sent-2411-072: Consequently, this VAE-based model is trained on the entirety of available protein family sequences.
elements.sent-2411-073: Subsequently, labeled sequences are encoded by the pretrained VAE into $d$-dimensional latent points, enabling the supervised training of a surrogate model, $\surrogate: \mathbb{R ^d \mapsto \mathbb{R}$, to predict fitness values and approximate the fitness landscape.}
elements.sent-2411-074: We go into detail regarding the architecture of the VAE used in our study.
elements.sent-2411-075: In our study, we leverage the powerful representation of the pre-trained 30-layer ESM-2 by making it the encoder of our model.
elements.sent-2411-076: Given an input sequence $s = \langle s_0, s_1, \cdots, s_L \rangle$, where $s_i \in \mathcal{A}$, ESM-2 computes representations for each token $s_i$ in $s$, resulting in a token-level hidden representation $H = \langle h_0, h_1, \cdots, h_L \rangle, h_i \in \mathbb{R ^{d_h}$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: We calculate the global representation $h \in \mathbb{R ^{d_h}$ of $s$ via a weighted sum of its tokens: \begin{equation}h = \sum_{i = 1} ^ L \frac{\omega ^ T \exp{(h_i)}}{\sum_{i = 1} ^ L \omega ^ T \exp{(h_i)}} h_i.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Then, two multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) are used to compute $\mu = \text{MLP _1(h)$ and $\log \sigma = \text{MLP _2(h)$, where the latent dimension is $d$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, a latent representation $x \in \mathbb{R ^d$ is sampled from $\mathcal{N (\mu, \sigma ^ 2)$, which is further proceeded to the decoder to reconstruct the sequence $\hat{s}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: To decode latent points into sequences, we employ a deep convolutional network as proposed by \citet{Castro2022 , consisting of four one-dimensional convolutional layers.}
elements.sent-2411-081: ReLU activations and batch normalization layers are applied between the convolutional layers, except for the final layer.
elements.sent-2411-082: We need to build a graph $G = (\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E )$ from the sequences in $\mathcal{D}$ to perform label propagation.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The graph's nodes are created by sampling $N$ sequences $s \in \mathcal{D}$, and the edges are constructed by $k$-nearest neighbors to the latent embeddings of $x = \phi(s)$, where $\phi$ is the pre-trained encoder.
elements.sent-2411-084: We introduce new nodes to $G$ by interpolating the learned latent $x$ with random noise $\epsilon \in \mathcal{N (0,I_d)$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We argue that Levenshtein distance is an inadequate metric for computing protein sequence similarity as done in \cite{kirjner2024improving because marginal amino acid variations can result in substantial fitness disparities \cite{MAYNARDSMITH1970,brookes2022sparsity .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Meanwhile, high-dimensional latent embeddings are effective at capturing implicit patterns within protein sequences.
elements.sent-2411-087: Consequently, Euclidean distance can serve as a suitable metric to quantify sequence similarity for constructing kNN graphs, as proteins with comparable properties (indicated by small fitness score differences) tend to cluster closely in the latent space.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{CreateGraph: Latent Graph Construction} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require training embeddings $\mathcal{V}$, $\#$of graph nodes $N$, constant $\beta$ \While{$\lvert \mathcal{V} \rvert < N$} \State $\overline x \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{V})$ \State $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_d)$ \Comment{Sample Gaussian noise $\epsilon$.} \State $z \xleftarrow{} \beta * \overline x + (1-\beta) * \epsilon$ \Comment{Interpolate between $ \overline x$ and $\epsilon$.} \State $\mathcal{V} \xleftarrow{} \mathcal{V} \cup \{z\}$ \EndWhile \State $\mathcal{E} \xleftarrow{} \bigcup_{x \in \mathcal{V}}kNN(x, \mathcal{V})$ \Comment{Construct edges (\Cref{algo:kNN})} \State \Return $G = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ \end{algorithmic} \label{algo:graph_construction}
elements.sent-2411-088: We consider a constructed graph G.
elements.sent-2411-089: For each node $v \in \mathcal{V}$, we assign a fitness label $y$, creating a set of fitness labels $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-090: This set $Y$ can be further decomposed into $Y = \{Y_n, Y_u\}$, representing the known and unknown labels, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-091: Known labels $Y_n$ are obtained from the training dataset, while unknown labels $Y_u$ are initialized by 0 and assigned to randomly generated nodes, as shown in line 3 of Algorithm~\ref{algo:smooth .}
elements.sent-2411-092: We then run the following label propagation in $m$ times. \begin{equation}Y^{\prime} = \alpha D^{-1/2} A D ^ {-1/2} Y + (1-\alpha) Y, \label{eq:label_propagation}\end{equation} here, $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is a weighted coefficient and $A$ is a weighted adjacency matrix, defined as: \begin{equation}A_{ij} = \begin{cases} \frac{\gamma}{d(x_i, x_j)} \text{if } i \neq j, 0 \text{if } i = j, \end{cases} \label{eq:weighted-adj}\end{equation} and $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the Euclidean distance, $\gamma$ is a controllable factor.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}A_{ij} =  \frac{\gamma}{d(x_i, x_j)} if  i \neq j, 0 if  i = j,  \label{eq:weighted-adj}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: In summary, \Cref{algo:smooth details our smoothing strategy by label propagation.}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Smooth: Latent Graph-based Smoothing} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require graph $G = (\mathcal V, \mathcal E)$, label propagation layers $m$ \State $A \leftarrow WeightedAdjacencyMatrix(\mathcal E)$ \Comment{\Cref{eq:weighted-adj}} \State $D \leftarrow DegreeMatrix(\mathcal V)$ \Comment{Compute degree matrix.} \State ${Y} \leftarrow {[0 \text{ if } is\_synthetic \text{ else } y_i]_{i=1}^N}$ \For{$i = 0,\ldots, m - 1$} \State $Y \leftarrow LabelPropagation(A,D,Y)$ \Comment{\Cref{eq:label_propagation}} \EndFor \State \Return $Y$ \end{algorithmic} \label{algo:smooth}
elements.sent-2411-094: This section delves into the theoretical foundations of our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-095: We begin by discussing the concepts of interpolation and extrapolation within high-dimensional latent embedding spaces.
elements.sent-2411-096: These concepts ensure the correctness of generating additional pseudo-samples within the latent space of a VAE-based model.
elements.sent-2411-097: Subsequently, leveraging the definition of convex hulls, we establish an upper bound for the expected distance between the generated nodes and the collection of training latent embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-098: This bound informs the intuition behind our kNN graph construction, detailed in ~\Cref{algo:graph_construction .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Consequently, employing label propagation within this bounded distance emerges as a rational strategy for assigning pseudo-labels to the newly generated nodes.
elements.sent-2411-100: We first list the definitions of a convex hull, interpolation, and extrapolation as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-101: Let $\mathbb{X = \{x_1, \dots, x_N \}$ be a set of $N$ points in $\mathbb{R ^d$, a convex hull of $\mathbb{X}$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\conv \triangleq \bigg\{\sum_{i=1}^N \lambda_i x_i | \lambda_i \ge 0 \text{ and } \sum_{i=1}^N \lambda_i = 1\bigg\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Given a set of $N$ training protein sequences $\mathbb{S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N\}$, we use the encoder $\phi$ of the pretrained VAE to compute their latent embeddings, resulting in a set of latent vectors $\mathbb{X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$, where $x_i = \phi(s_i)$ (see \Cref{sec:vae ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: In this paper, we consider the scenario wherein we have limited access to experimental data, and we train a VAE-based model to represent continuous $d$-dimensional latent vectors of those available sequences.
elements.sent-2411-104: This, therefore, gives rise to two mild assumptions in our work.
elements.sent-2411-105: Assumption 1: $N$ is significantly smaller than the exponential in $d$, where $N$ represents the number of data samples.
elements.sent-2411-106: Assumption 2: Each latent vector $x$ is sampled from $\mathcal{N (0, I_d)$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: This is achieved by regularizing the model with $KL$ divergence loss as shown in the \Cref{eq:vae_loss .}
elements.sent-2411-108: We assess \methodname's extrapolation capabilities by computing the probability of its generated samples falling outside the convex hull of the training data within the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-109: Subsequently, we derive an upper bound for these reliable extrapolation regions.
elements.sent-2411-110: These findings are formalized in the following propositions.
elements.sent-2411-111: Let $\mathbb{X = \{x_i \in \mathbb{R ^d\}_{i=1 ^N$ be a set of $N$ i.i.d $d$-dimensional samples from $\mathcal{N (0,I_d)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Assume that $N \ll \exp \big(\frac{d {2(C^2_{\beta +2)}\big)$ and let $z = \beta * \overline x + (1-\beta) * \epsilon$ for some $\overline x \in \mathbb{X}$, $\beta \in (0,1)$, $C_{\beta = \frac{1+\beta {1-\beta}$ and $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0,I_d)$, then \begin{equation}\lim_{d \rightarrow \infty} \mathbf{P}(z \notin \conv) = 1.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: We leave the proof of the presented proposition in \Cref{app:proof_of_prob_limit .}
elements.sent-2411-114: Based on \Cref{def:interpolation , this theoretical result guarantees the correctness of our formula shown in line 4 of \Cref{algo:graph_construction in expanding the latent space of protein sequences.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: However, when generated nodes are located far from the training convex hull, their embedding vectors differ significantly from those in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-116: This disparity hinders the identification of meaningful similarities and can result in unreliable fitness scores determined through label propagation.
elements.sent-2411-118: For any $\overline{x \in \mathbb{X}$, we define $z = \beta * \overline x + (1-\beta) * \epsilon$ with $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0,I_d), \beta \in (0,1)$, the following holds: \begin{equation}\mathbb{E}[D(z,\conv)] < 2(1-\beta)\sqrt{d},\end{equation} where $D(z, \conv)) \triangleq \inf_{x \in \conv d(z, x)$ is the distance from a point $z \in \mathbb{R ^d$ to $\conv$, and $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the Euclidean distance.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: The proof of this proposition can be found in \Cref{app:prop_distance .}
elements.sent-2411-120: The derived upper bound increases linearly w.r.t square root of the latent dimension $d$ with a rate of $2(1-\beta)$.
elements.sent-2411-121: It is worth noting that $\beta$ is used to control the exploration rate of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-122: As $\beta \rightarrow 1$, the generated node $z$ closely resembles a training node $\overline x$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Conversely, a lower $\beta$ introduces more noise into the latent vectors, resulting in samples that are further from the source nodes.
elements.sent-2411-124: This upper bound ensures that synthetic nodes remain within a controllable "reliable zone", making label propagation a sensible choice, as the label values of these synthetic nodes cannot deviate too far from the overall distribution of existing nodes.
elements.sent-2411-125: In summary, Propositions \ref{prop:prob_limit and \ref{prop:distance offer theoretical underpinnings for our latent-based smoothing approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: By mapping discrete sequences to a continuous $d$-dimensional latent space, we establish a quantitative relationship between newly added and original training nodes, governed by parameters $d$ and $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-127: This quantitative connection enhances the interpretability of our method.
elements.sent-2411-128: Latent space model-based optimization (MBO) employs surrogate models, $f_\Phi: \mathbb{R ^d \mapsto \mathbb{R}$, to efficiently guide the search for optimal values that maximize expensive black-box functions.}
elements.sent-2411-129: The effectiveness of MBO depends on the accuracy of the surrogate model as poorly trained surrogates can hinder the optimization process by providing misleading information.
elements.sent-2411-130: As shown in \Cref{algo:groot , lines 1, 4, and 5 encompass the standard MBO process of encoding training vectors into latent space and training a surrogate model to predict fitness scores Y based on these embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Meanwhile, lines 2 and 3 are two additional steps introduced by \method before surrogate training.
elements.sent-2411-132: Moreover, \method can be used in tandem with any surrogate models as it only accesses the data representations.
elements.sent-2411-133: These two properties make \method a versatile approach for optimization tasks in a wide range of domains.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{\method} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require training dataset $\mathcal{D}$, pre-trained encoder $\phi$, $\#$ of graph nodes $N$, constant $\beta$, label propagation layers $m$ \State $\mathcal{V} \leftarrow \{x_i = \phi(s_i), s_i \in \mathcal{D}\}_{i=1}^n$ \Comment{Compute latent embeddings.} \State $\mathcal V', \mathcal E \leftarrow CreateGraph(\mathcal V, N, \beta)$ \Comment{\Cref{algo:graph_construction}} \State $\hat Y \leftarrow Smooth\big((\mathcal V', \mathcal E), L \big)$ \Comment{\Cref{algo:smooth}} \State $\Phi \leftarrow \arg\min_{\Phi} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim (\mathcal V', \hat Y)} \big[ (y - f_\Phi(x))^2 \big]$ \Comment{Train surrogate model.} \State $X^*, Y^* \leftarrow MBO(\Phi, \mathcal V')$ \Comment{Model-based optimization.} \State \Return $X^*, Y^*$ \end{algorithmic} \label{algo:groot}
elements.sent-2411-134: We demonstrate the benefits of our proposed \method on two protein tasks with varying levels of label scarcity.
elements.sent-2411-135: Furthermore, since obtaining the ground-truth fitness of generated sequences is costly, we further evaluate our method on Design-Bench \cite{pmlr-v162-trabucco22a .}
elements.sent-2411-136: These benchmarks have exact oracles, providing a better validation of our method's performance on real-world datasets.

elements.sent-2411-138: While most methods perform well, \method achieves the highest average score in maximum performance and is slightly behind the SOTA baseline in median and mean benchmarks.

elements.sent-2411-148: In addition to these algorithms, we also benchmark against some most recent methods: GFN-AL \cite{pmlr-v162-jain22a , ReLSO \cite{Castro2022 , PEX \cite{pmlr-v162-ren22a , and GGS \cite{kirjner2024improving , utilizing and modifying the code provided by their respective authors.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Furthermore, we assess ReLSO enhanced with our smoothing strategy for better optimization, termed S-ReLSO.
elements.sent-2411-150: We use three metrics proposed by \citet{pmlr-v162-jain22a : fitness, diversity, and novelty.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Let the optimized sequences $\mathcal G^* = \{ g_1^*, \ldots, g_K^* \}$.
elements.sent-2411-152: Fitness is defined as the median of the evaluated fitness of $K = 128$ proposed designs.
elements.sent-2411-153: Diversity is the median of the distances between every pair of sequences in $\mathcal G^*$.
elements.sent-2411-154: Novelty is defined as the median of the distances between every generated sequences to the training set.
elements.sent-2411-155: Mathematical definitions of these metrics are defined in the \Cref{app:metrics .}
elements.sent-2411-156: For each task, we use the full dataset $\mathcal D^*$ to train the VAE, whose architecture has been described in \Cref{sec:vae .}
elements.sent-2411-157: We utilize the pretrained checkpoint esm2\_t30\_150M\_UR50D\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/facebook/esm2_t30_150M_UR50D of the ESM-2 model, fine-tuning only the last layer while freezing the rest.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: The latent representation space dimension is set to $d = 320$.

elements.sent-2411-160: It is important to note that these hyperparameters are not finely tuned.

elements.sent-2411-162: After the smoothing process, the features and refined labels are inputted into a surrogate model, specifically a shallow 2-layer multi-layers perceptron (MLP) with a dropout rate of $0.2$ in the first layer, to train a smoothed surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-163: Despite its simplicity, we consider MLP a suitable choice due to its proven effectiveness in previous studies \cite{huang2021combining .}
elements.sent-2411-164: For model-based optimization, we select two gradient-based algorithms to exploit the smoothness of the surrogate model: Gradient Ascent (GA) with a learning rate of $0.005$ for $400$ iterations and Limited-memory Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (L-BFGS) \cite{Liu1989 for a maximum of $6$ iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-165: While various optimization algorithms are available, we choose these gradient-based methods based on the premise that they can effectively leverage the smoothness of the surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-166: It is crucial to note that our primary focus is not on evaluating multiple optimization methods, but rather on demonstrating the effectiveness of the smoothing strategy.
elements.sent-2411-167: We empirically evaluate these two algorithms and report the best results.
elements.sent-2411-168: We report the mean and standard deviation of the evaluation metrics over five runs with different random seeds in \Cref{tab:numeric .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Firstly, compared to the best fitness of the training data (\Cref{tab:stats ), our method successfully optimizes all tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-170: For AAV tasks, our method achieves SOTA results across all difficulty levels.
elements.sent-2411-171: Notably, although our method only slightly exceeds AdaLead in the harder2 task, \method generates sequences whose novelty falls within the mutation gap range, indicating appropriate extrapolation and a higher likelihood of producing synthesizable proteins.
elements.sent-2411-172: Additionally, applying our smoothing strategy to ReLSO improves the model's performance by $60\%$ in the harder1, $64.7\%$ in the harder2, and $22.7\%$ in the harder3 tasks.
elements.sent-2411-173: For GFP tasks, \method outperforms all baseline methods across all difficulties, especially in the harder3 difficulty, where GGS failed to optimize properly.
elements.sent-2411-174: It is crucial to mention that ReLSO failed to generate diverse sequences in GFP tasks, as indicated by zero diversity across five different runs.
elements.sent-2411-175: Overall, we observe that \method achieves the highest fitness while maintaining respectable diversity and novelty.
elements.paragraph-2411-013: Impact of smoothing on extrapolation and general performance
elements.sent-2411-176: For each benchmark, we assess the impact of smoothing on extrapolation capabilities by measuring the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of the surrogate model on the benchmark's training and holdout datasets relative to the experimental ground-truth.
elements.sent-2411-177: Our results demonstrate that smoothing reduces MAE for both the training and holdout sets.
elements.sent-2411-178: This reduction occurs because our smoothing strategy acts as a data augmentation technique in the latent space, enhancing the robustness of the supervised model.
elements.sent-2411-179: We also observe that the MAE for both sets increases gradually as the task becomes more difficult.
elements.sent-2411-180: We hypothesize that this is due to the smaller training set size in more difficult tasks, requiring a greater number of new samples to construct a graph.
elements.sent-2411-181: While the labels of these new samples are not exact, they help to smooth the latent landscape, resulting in a smoother but not perfectly accurate surrogate model, which in turn increases the MAE.
elements.sent-2411-182: Additionally, \Cref{tab:smooth-fitness demonstrates how a smoothed model dramatically outperforms its unsmoothed counterpart across all tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-183: For AAV, the results show up to a threefold improvement compared to the unsmoothed surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-184: For GFP, without smoothing, the optimization algorithm struggles to find a maximum point, resulting in suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-185: With our smoothing technique, the optimization algorithm can optimize properly.
elements.sent-2411-186: Our findings indicate that our smoothing strategy enables gradient-based optimization methods to better navigate the search space and identify superior maxima.
elements.sent-2411-187: However, we also observe that applying smoothing decreases diversity.
elements.sent-2411-188: This can be explained by the optimization process converging, causing the population to concentrate in certain high-fitness regions.
elements.sent-2411-189: In contrast, without proper optimization, the population diverges and spreads throughout the search space.

elements.sent-2411-192: For each VAE version, we construct a graph and apply the smoothing technique as described in \Cref{sec:smoothing to generate new nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Secondly, to determine whether the generated nodes lie within the convex hull, we check if each point can be expressed as a convex combination of the training points.

elements.paragraph-2411-015: Explore the limits of smoothing strategy
elements.sent-2411-200: In this section, we investigate the smoothing capabilities of our method by testing its limits.
elements.sent-2411-201: We use the GFP harder3 task and the AAV harder3 task as examined cases, further subsampling the dataset with ratios $r \in \{ 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0 \}$ in two different ways, namely: random and lowest.
elements.sent-2411-202: In the random setting, data points are randomly subsampled with the given ratio $r$, while in the lowest setting, we use the fraction $r$ of the dataset with the lowest fitness scores.
elements.sent-2411-203: Using the same hyperparameters, \Cref{fig:limit shows the median performance of \method on AAV and GFP w.r.t the ratio $r$ in both settings.}
elements.sent-2411-204: In the random setting, our method outperforms the best data point in the entire harder3 dataset using only $20\%$ of the labeled data, which amounts to fewer than $100$ samples per set.
elements.sent-2411-205: In the lowest setting, the method requires $50\%$ of the labeled data, approximately $200$ samples per set, to achieve similar performance.
elements.sent-2411-206: This demonstrates that our method is effective even under extreme conditions with limited labeled data.

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{kNN: k-Nearest Neighbors} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Current node $x$, All nodes $\mathcal V$ \State $D(x) \leftarrow \bigcup_{x' \in \mathcal V/\{x\}} ||x' - x||$ \Comment{Euclidean distance.} \State $\mathcal X' \leftarrow TopK(D(x),\mathcal V)$ \Comment{Retrieve K closest nodes to $x$.} \State $\mathcal E(x) \leftarrow \bigcup_{x' \in \mathcal X'}(x,x')$ \Comment{Construct neighborhood around $x$.} \State \Return $\mathcal E(x)$ \end{algorithmic} \label{algo:kNN}
elements.section-2411-008: Numerical Results For Other Protein Tasks
elements.sent-2411-236: In this section, we present experimental results of other difficulties of two protein datasets: GFP and AAV.
